one_line_takeaway: Classic DNN neuron-coverage criteria transfer to LLMs when measured at the right place (NC/TKNC on Attention layers at the last query token), and the resulting activated-neuron signal powers a robust real-time jailbreak detector (93.61% avg) that beats perplexity/PARDEN baselines across diverse attack families.
key_finding: Neuron coverage differs significantly between normal and jailbreak queries, and this signal is actionable. NC and TKNC are the most stable/effective criteria (TKNP, TFC, NLC vary strongly across model architecture/size); Attention layers capture query features better than MLP layers; and the last token of the original query is the most informative measurement point -- generating extra tokens does NOT improve discrimination and in larger models actually reduces it. The activated-neuron-count detector reaches 93.61% average accuracy and, unlike perplexity/PARDEN/Self-Reminder baselines, stays robust across GCG, DeepInception, Masterkey, and JailBreakV attacks. Coverage-guided jailbreak generation clearly beats random seed selection.
core_contribution: An empirical study repurposing traditional DNN white-box coverage criteria (originally designed for small neural networks) to LLMs, using jailbreak attacks as the driving abnormal-behavior scenario. The paper's OWN contributions are: (1) a systematic evaluation of five coverage criteria (NC, TKNC, TKNP, TFC, NLC) across three dimensions -- criterion level, model-layer level, and generated-token level -- finding that Neuron Coverage (NC) and Top-K Neuron Coverage (TKNC) are the most effective/stable, that Attention layers outperform MLP layers, and that measuring at the last query token is best; and (2) three downstream security applications built on these findings: a real-time jailbreak detector (MLP classifier over per-block activated-neuron counts), coverage-based test-case prioritization, and coverage-guided jailbreak-case generation. NOTE: NC, KMNC, TKNC/TKNP, TFC, NLC, SmoothLLM, PARDEN, perplexity filters, Self-Reminder etc. are all PRIOR/cited work; the paper's novelty is adapting and evaluating these criteria in the LLM setting and the three applications, not inventing the coverage metrics themselves.
threat_model: Jailbreak attacks against aligned LLMs: adversaries craft prompts (adversarial GCG suffixes, template/instruction-based attacks like DeepInception and Masterkey, and JailBreakV-style templates) that bypass safety alignment to elicit harmful/disallowed content. The paper treats such jailbreak-induced abnormal behavior as a 'fault' analogous to adversarial misclassification in DNN testing. The defender is assumed to have white-box access to the model's internal hidden states / neuron activations, which it uses to (a) detect jailbreak queries in real time from the first output token, (b) prioritize high-risk test cases, and (c) generate new jailbreak cases for robustness testing.
limitations: Authors note (Threats to Validity) generalizability limits from chosen settings and reproduction fidelity of baselines (perplexity filter, PARDEN, GCG, DeepInception, Masterkey, Self-Reminder), mitigated by repeating key experiments >3x. Additional reviewer-observed limits: the study explicitly focuses on coverage-change TRENDS rather than exact values, and NC thresholds T are set per model empirically (0.1/0.25/0.75/50), so criteria are not parameter-free. The detector and prioritization applications are validated on only three of the four models (OPT-125M, Llama-2-7B-Chat, Pythia-12B; Gemma-2-27B-it omitted from applications), and jailbreak-case generation is a 'preliminary exploration' on a single model (Llama-2-7b-chat) with a single random baseline. NLC is computed without training-data prior knowledge (unavailable), which the authors flag as altering its behavior. Two of four models (OPT-125M, Pythia-12B) are non-safety-aligned, so 'jailbreak' there is a proxy for abnormal-knowledge activation rather than alignment bypass.

datasets_used: Alpaca-GPT4 (source of normal queries; 1,500 base + 2,500 train / 500 test for detector)
JailBreakV-28k (rejected+attack query pairs; 2,500 train / 500 test attack queries after filtering GCG)
AdvBench (rejected/malicious queries; also perplexity-filter threshold source)
TruthfulQA (817-query generalization validation set for normal queries)
Synonymous Query Dataset (GPT-4 paraphrases of first 500 Alpaca-GPT4 queries, manually verified)
GCG-generated attacks (200 validation), DeepInception (50 validation), Masterkey (200 validation)
quantitative_results: Real-time jailbreak detector average accuracy: 95.23% (OPT-125M), 93.92% (Llama-2-7B-Chat), 91.69% (Pythia-12B); overall ~93.61% average
Detector on Llama-2-7B-Chat per-set: Alpaca-gpt4 95.60%, JailBreakV 97.00%, TruthfulQA 91.43%, GCG 86.00%, DeepInception 100.00%, Masterkey 93.50%
Requirement-1 (redundancy) avg Attention-layer coverage growth SNS vs SN: NC 3.16%, TKNC 7.50%, TKNP 33.02%, TFC 24.09%, NLC 2.77% (NC/TKNC/NLC pass; TKNP/TFC over-sensitive)
Requirement-2 (attack sensitivity) avg Attention-layer growth SNJ vs SN: NC 15.23%, TKNC 17.37%, TKNP 32.99%, TFC 45.88%, NLC 44.03%
Requirement-3 stability: NC/TKNC lowest variance (max 0.01 for TKNC MLP on SNJ); NLC variance 0.20 and TFC 0.07 on SNJ Attention
Test-case prioritization avg accuracy: 84.60% (OPT-125M), 90.60% (Llama-2-7B-Chat), 83.60% (Pythia-12B); attack-case detection up to 97.80% (Llama) and 94.60% (Pythia)
Coverage-guided jailbreak generation successful queries over rounds 1-5: 6, 11, 19, 24, 32 vs random 2, 2, 6, 9, 12
Storing hidden states for 8,000 queries = 5.2 TB (0.0002% of training data), motivating dropping full-training-data criteria

Baseline detector avg accuracy (Llama-2-7B-Chat): Perplexity-Sentence 47.86%, Perplexity-Window 47.54%, PARDEN-Sentence 67.23%, PARDEN-Window 67.60%, Self-Reminder 73.63%, Cluster 76.80% -- all below the paper's 93.92%
baselines_compared: Clustering-based detector (built by the authors from their empirical-study k-means method)
Random seed-selection strategy (baseline for coverage-guided jailbreak generation)
